sentences.text: Well, the-- the Germans-- I don't know, there was a bakery somewhere.
And they baked some sort of bread, which was bad.
But they gave for each person-- can't remember how much bread.
You know, a portion of bread every day.
You had to go and get it somewhere.
As you know, we had Ukrainians all around us.
Some of-- some were not too bad.

So we used to barter with them, give them if we still had something left over, you know, whatever, like clothes or-- money wasn't worth anything-- maybe a piece of jewelry, or something like that, or some household item.
In the beginning, it wasn't that strict.
You could get out once in a while.
But later on, you couldn't move.
They were-- we were just surrounded.
Like leaving that, you couldn't move anywhere.
And we were hungry.
We were starving.
Some people did starve.
But--
Very bad.
Very bad.
In our case, I think we lived in the ghetto only one winter.
And in our case, we were lucky, because we had lots of trees in our yard.
So we used to chop up some trees.
People took apart fences.
You know, you did whatever you could to survive.
Yeah.
Well, not very much.
You know, as I say, once in a while, a bit of potato or a few beans.
Not much.
We didn't have very much.
Yes.
The first year, we observed just about every holiday.
We had-- it was just a few houses past our house.
It was a big house.
And the people made a meal in there.
And people used to go and pray.
They closed off all the windows and everything, used to go and pray.
I remember exactly the first Rosh Hashanah in the ghetto.
And then we still had a little bit of food.
And I remember my mother making some, I think, maybe chicken soup even.
And you know, shutting all the windows, closing it up, making dark so nobody sees you.
But we did have-- we observed, as bad as things were.
No.
We couldn't have any matzah.
Yeah.
We all had to work.
Well, that's where I worked is with this uncle in the--
Yeah.
And also, they had-- they needed wool, the Germans, to make wool, because it was so Cold.
They were freezing.
So we learned to spin wool.
Some people knew those things, and then they set up spinning wheels, and they taught us how to do it.
And we used to spin wool.
Other women were knitting, you know, and sort of things like that.
I mean, we didn't have fun, believe me.
But we did have-- you know, we were sort of a comara-- camaraderie--
Hard to say.
We-- we had that.
We used to talk a lot with each other.
We used to dream of things.
What are we going to do, how are we going to do it?
A lot of people had hope to run away, to plot, to do things.
And as you'll see later when I tell you, some people did run away from the ghetto, and they did save themselves.
And so we were doing all those things.
And of course, we still had some books to read.
But we used to talk a lot, discuss things.
From the world?
Nothing, nothing, nothing.
We didn't have anything like that.
We were just too small.
You know, radio just started coming in in those days just before the war.
It wasn't developed like it is here, so we didn't have any news.
But everybody used to think and wonder why the Germans are so mean to us.

What did we ever do to them to be-- and they were all hoping that Roosevelt-- well, they remembered him, that he was the president-- that he won't let, after all, everybody die, that he will send some help.
But of course, never came.
Yes, there was hope, ever time-- to the last minute, I think, there was hope.
Well, the first [NON-ENGLISH], they used to call it, came in [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] spring.
It was Shabbat, I remember that.
I think it was '42, maybe.
And what happened then, they went in.
The SS came in the middle of night, and they went to every house.
They picked out whoever they saw and took them away and killed them.
Took them-- in our case, it was outside of town, a few miles outside.
They made them dig the graves.
And then they let them stand on the end of the road, and they shot them.
I don't remember how many people.
I don't think that there is a statistic about that.
But as I say, everybody that they found, they did.
In mine case, my family, we went down to our cellar with the other occupants of the house, and we all sat there.
They walked into the house.
The house was empty.
They didn't look too close, and they didn't found us.
And the next day, you could walk out, and you were free.
And nothing happened to you.
They were just a fast [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], the way you-- it means.
And then we survived this whole summer yet.
Of course, we found who was killed.
I mean, it was terrible for us to see what family, friends-- but nevertheless, we survived, and we kept on living till the same year in the fall, in Sukkot.
Yes, it was-- and from then on, we were really guarded.
You couldn't get out.
You couldn't get in.
You couldn't-- they didn't even want you to work anymore.
You were, like, you know--
Just sat there and wait for them to come and to kill you.
So we lived there till fall.
And in fall was the liquidation of the ghetto.
And that was in still in '42, the fall of '42.
I mean, things happened so fast.
It only took months, sometimes days.
So-- so they surrounded us.
And they also took young, strong men to dig graves the night before.
And we knew that that's the end of us.
We really knew.
It was-- now that I can think of it, it was so sad that here you are, young people, old people, children, that you know, for what reason you should-- you have to die.
And I must say that the will to live was so strong in everybody.
And me, I think I just wanted to live.
I almost didn't care about anything else.
But I wanted to save myself.
But there was no way to do it.
Closed-- blocked with the entrance, with blocks.
You know, they-- they set it up like that.
And so it was sort of safe for a day or two.
But how many days can you live like that, with no air, with no food, with no water?
So I think we survived, I don't know, a few days.
I can't remember how many, but some days.
But then we had to get out.
There was no way you could-- you will die there, or you die here.
It's the same thing.
My father and my two brothers, they stayed in the house, and they were taken out.
And they-- they died with everybody else.
But me and my mother and my sister, we survived a few days.
But then they-- they opened this place, and we all got out.
And as soon as somebody-- it was at night, but I don't know how they noticed.
As soon as they got out, we heard shots.
People they start-- the Ukrainians start shooting, shooting.
And I think they got everybody.
But I managed-- I don't know how I did it.
I think it was a miracle.
I went under a bush somewhere, and they didn't see me.
They walked past me, and they didn't see me.
And I was laying there.
Then when I heard it quiet down, they're all gone, I crawled into a barn.
There was a barn not far from there.
And I laid there on the haystack.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Early morning, somebody came in to get the horses, and they saw me.
A man, a Ukrainian.
So he didn't say a word to me, but I knew what he's going to do.
But I have no choice.
I mean, I can't run.
Where am I going to run?
So I stayed there.
Because they went around, and whoever they caught, the next day, they shot them.
But because it was a Sunday, they weren't going to do the job on Sunday.
They were drinking and having a good time.
So we was standing there all day.
Like pardon?
In the-- in the basement there where they took us in, like we were prisoners, right?
Where they took us in, I saw mine dad's jacket.
And I was sure that he must have been there too.
They had him in that same basement.
No, no.
It was just laying there.
[DRINKS] And they were-- I was the only girl there, woman.
There were more-- the other people were men, and, you know, young, strong men.
And they were plotting to get away.
And I was the only one-- they didn't want to take me-- that I survived that ordeal.
And I kept on running, and running in the middle of the night.
I didn't know where I was running.
I just knew the direction where I was going.
But I didn't know what it was there or anything.
I ran and ran until about early hours of the morning.
I found myself in front of a house.
And not knowing what else to do, I knocked on the door.
And I thought maybe they'll have pity on me.
And as it turned out, they did.
They let me in.
They-- they saw who I was.
They let me in.
They were Ukrainians.
And they--
Yes, definitely.
They gave me something to eat.
And they-- they heard.
I mean, it was close.
How much can you run?
A few kilometers-- I mean, I couldn't run who knows how far.
So they know what's going on.
Maybe-- maybe, maybe.
It was nothing concrete.
Nobody could tell you what to do.
So I had to go.
Of course, I went.
I was grateful for what they did for me.
And I went into the woods there.
And I-- there was a couple with a child from another town.
But they made it up to this place.
No, I'm sorry.
I don't know.
I think in those days, I did.
But I don't remember.
And I-- when I saw this couple, I-- I was really overcome with grief.
But at the same time, I was happy to have a soul to talk to, to say a word to.
And I stayed with them most of the day until it was evening.
They said, you know, the baby cries, because the baby's hungry.
And it was a baby, maybe a year old.
And they said, for your own good, I think you should move on, not stay with us.
And there-- and I was sitting there, and it was towards evening already. and there was a Ukrainian girl walking with two cows.
And she saw me, and she of course knew who I was.
Ukrainian.
And-- But I mean, I have no-- what can I say?
What can I do?
I took it as a nice gesture.
And she went, and she came back.
And she said the mother allowed her to bring me home.
And she took me home, and I was with them for at least a month.
They kept me in a room.
They used to give me food.
They used to come in and talk to me.
And in the evening, I used to go out to their kitchen and sat with them.
Yes, they did.
They definitely did risk their life.
But they was very good people.
And they lost-- she-- they lost their father and a son to the Soviets.
They took them away to Siberia, because I guess they were wealthy farmers.
So she was so-- very religious too.
And she was just feeling sorry for me.
No, I don't remember the name.
I don't.
Well, the girl, I had her identity after.
They gave me her certificate.
I think it was a birth certificate.
They came up with-- I mean, I could-- one day, a few German soldiers drove up to the house, and they went into the house.
And I was in the house with another woman.
I don't know what they came for, but I was-- got very scared, and I ran out through the window into the field.
They didn't see me.
Nothing happened.
But it was a warning that you can't stay there any longer, for them and for me.
And they-- I don't if they bought him or whatever, but he kept quiet.
And he was agreeable to take me with him to friends into Russia, because this was all on the Russian border.
So we-- and so they told me that that's what they think I should do, and that might help me.
They gave me something to wear, some old boots.
And-- yes.
The name was Raya Terpelec, the girl's name.
And so I had something.
You know, it wasn't much, but it was something.
And at that time, I took on a gentile identity.
Yeah, with the name.
And I walked with him, and--
Yes, yes.
I felt very comfortable.
Well, I think I was blonde-- another thing, I know I was very blond, and blue eyes and fair skin.
And I don't think so I looked a typical Jewish person, you know, I could have gone.
Yeah, I could.
I mean, I was fluent in Ukrainian.
I knew Russian a bit.
I knew Polish, of course.
So languages were no problem.
So anyway, we walked, set out walking.
We walked a fair distance.

And then, because he was wearing this army uniform, and he was an army man, some of the trucks that when they were all-- mostly Russian-- you know, the Germans took in the prison, prisoners driving trucks.
So they gave him a ride.
And they brought us into this town.
It's called Novograd-Volynskii.
It was-- at the time, it was already German, I guess.
But it was in the Russian, of course.
And we went there to-- they let us off somewhere outside of the city, and we walked up.
He had friends there, this fellow.
So he said he'll take me there.
And we went there, and they let me stay the night over there.
They fed us supper.
And the next morning, he was going back where he came from, and I was on my own.
And I started walking the streets without knowing what to do.
I mean, those people, they didn't have it much themselves, and they wasn't going to keep me there.
I mean, they didn't know that I was Jewish anymore.
I wasn't already.
A line-up.
People are lined up.
And there are-- well, I think there are freight cars.
And I thought to myself, maybe that will be my rescue.
Maybe that's what I should do.
Because we were talking about things like that.
We knew that they are taking Ukrainians, Poles to work in Germany.
So I understood that that must be the thing.
But I mean, nobody came and said, I want to go with you.
I mean, they caught them and brought them, and they all went against their will.
But I thought, well, I'll just stand in the line.
They don't know.
And I stood in the line, and I was going.
And they took me.
I had presented them with this piece of paper.
No, they didn't.
What do they care?
It's a person with two hands, and maybe they'll need one of those.
They took me in.
And-- and the train got all filled up with-- full of people.
But it wasn't like the trains that took people to Auschwitz, I'm telling you.
Because some Gentiles want to sort of equate themselves with what happened to Jews.
It was not.
It was freight train.
Some had benches, some didn't.
But they gave them food.
They treated them more or less like human beings.
But we were not treated like humans anywhere, nowhere.
So I don't think I remember, because it was day and night, till we went back, like, didn't go into Russia, but went back to Poland and into Germany.
Yeah, they-- you know, rations.
Everything was rationed in those days.
But they give you something to keep you alive.
Bread, probably, some sort of coffee and maybe soup, you know.
Not great stuff.
And we arrived in this city.
It used to be Poland before, Katowice.
But now, of course, it was German.
And actually, Katowice is not far from this camp that we talk so much about, Auschwitz.
It's in that area.
And this part of Poland had a lot of folks, Germans.
They were Germans from before the war.
Then they were Polish when it was Poland.
And now they came back to be Germans.
They were vicious people.
That's why they had all the camps in that area.
And I think they were well-off.
There was a pretty nice lady, and he was a big fat German-looking German.
And they got me there, but I must say that they weren't too bad to me.
They were, you know-- I mean, there was food.
There was a decent place to sleep.
They used to get help, like cleaning ladies to come in, and this cleaning lady was telling me all the stories about Auschwitz.
She crossed herself for all the Jews that are dying there.
And she had been telling me all the stories.
And she doesn't know who I am, who she's telling it, how much it hurts me.
Because it was near.
I mean, word got around.
They knew what was happening.
It's not that they didn't know.
Everybody knew what was happening.
Being at that train going to Germany, all of a sudden, I see a friend of mine on that train.
And she ran away with Aryan papers before they liquidated the ghetto, and she was with some Ukrainians living.
But then of course, you know, they couldn't.
And she got the same idea, and she came on that same train.
And I can't tell you, when I saw her.
You know--
